critic_raw:
The central methodological flaw is a confounded comparison. The multi-task deep learning model receives both structured features AND unstructured clinical notes (TF-IDF + BioBERT embeddings), while the logistic regression baseline receives structured features only. Any observed performance difference cannot be attributed to the model architecture—it may entirely be due to the additional information in clinical notes, which the baseline never sees.

A valid comparison would require: (a) a logistic regression trained on the same structured + unstructured features, or (b) a deep learning model trained on structured features only.

Second, stratified random sampling across 60 months allows the same patient's multiple admissions to span train and test—patient-level leakage.

Third, the auxiliary LOS task shares layers with readmission prediction. LOS is correlated with readmission risk and may encode readmission-adjacent information.
all_issues_raised: Confounded comparison: DL model receives structured + unstructured data while LR baseline receives structured data only
Stratified random split across 60 months allows same patient across train/test splits — patient-level leakage
Auxiliary task (LOS regression) shares layers with readmission prediction; LOS may encode readmission-adjacent information
No statistical significance testing on AUROC differences

round_transcripts.critic:
The central flaw is a confounded comparison: DL receives structured + clinical notes; LR receives structured only. Any AUROC gap cannot be attributed to architecture—it may entirely reflect the additional data modality.

Second, stratified random split on 60 months of hospital data allows patient-level leakage—same patient's multiple admissions span train and test.

Third, auxiliary LOS shares layers with readmission prediction and LOS is correlated with readmission risk.
round_transcripts.defender:
On the confounded comparison: the design deliberately tests the full multi-modal deep learning pipeline against the current standard of care—the clinically relevant comparison. What does a hospital gain by deploying a deep learning system using clinical notes compared to their existing structured-data model? This is a stated design choice per the hypothesis.

On patient-level splitting: the design uses 'all index admissions (first admission in a rolling 60-day window).' This rolling window constraint limits each patient to at most one admission per 60-day period, substantially reducing patient-level overlap. Department-level stratification controls for the most systematic confounds.

On auxiliary task: LOS is defined at discharge—it is available at prediction time. Using LOS as an auxiliary signal is standard multi-task learning practice and does not constitute label leakage.

round_transcripts.critic:
The 'clinically relevant' framing does not resolve the validity problem—it redefines the question. A positive result proves only that (DL + clinical notes) > (LR + structured data), not that DL architecture provides value. This means a positive result is nearly predetermined—adding more informative features to a more complex model will almost always improve prediction.

On patient splitting: the 60-day rolling window reduces but does not eliminate patient-level overlap. A patient admitted in month 1 (training) and month 4 (test) exceeds the 60-day window and would both be included. The design makes no statement about patient-level deduplication across splits.

The LOS auxiliary task concern is withdrawn—LOS known at discharge is a legitimate auxiliary signal.
round_transcripts.defender:
I concede that the 60-day rolling window does not prevent the same patient appearing in both splits if their admissions are separated by more than 60 days. A stricter patient-level split exclusion would be needed. This is a legitimate design gap.

On the confounded comparison: I maintain this is the intended comparison per the hypothesis, but acknowledge it limits causal attribution. A positive result cannot conclude 'DL architecture is superior'—only that the combined system outperforms the baseline.

round_transcripts.critic: The defender has conceded both the patient-level split issue and the interpretive limitation of the confounded comparison. The confounded comparison is not merely an interpretive limitation—it means the study cannot answer its stated hypothesis. If the entire performance gain comes from clinical notes, a logistic regression with clinical notes would perform equally well. The experiment as designed cannot distinguish this scenario.
round_transcripts.defender: I accept that both issues remain substantively unresolved. The experiment tests a combined intervention (architecture + data) against a baseline, limiting causal attribution. The patient-level split gap is real. These are material limitations that the design should address through an ablation (LR on structured + clinical notes) and explicit patient-level exclusion.

all_issues_adjudicated: Confounded comparison: architecture and data modality are co-varied; cannot attribute performance to model architecture
Stratified random split allows same patient across train/test splits — patient-level leakage unaddressed
